data.title: Tesla: EV Market Pressures, Rising Competition, And Musk's Branding Crisis
data.description:
Are we seeing the fall of Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) or the fall of Elon Musk, or is it the other way around?

The truth is that the two things are connected, as the company is going through one of the most serious crises in its history, which has already caused its market value to fall by half in just a few months.

And yes, arguably, market correction and stretched valuations are the key factors behind this. But it's also important to make it clear that many of the factors that are causing the stock to plummet now are quite different from other moments in the company's history.

And that's what I believe investors need to understand, where I break down what's happening to Tesla into three major impacts, ranging from (1) structural changes in the global electric vehicle market; (2) competition with Chinese automakers; and (3) the last of which is linked to the company's leadership, its CEO Elon Musk.

The confluence of these three factors, in my opinion, tends to drive Tesla into the abyss, where it may be very difficult for robotaxi, FSD, and robotics to rescue it—especially in the short to medium term, at the very least.

While it seems intriguing and perhaps even somewhat paradoxical, I believe that as long as Elon Musk continues to repel Tesla's customers, and consequently destroy the reputation of the Tesla brand, the stock is likely to reflect it underperforming the markets.

For there to be a reversal of this bearish trend, perhaps turning Tesla into a SaaS (software-as-a-service) company as soon as possible could be crucial, and could perhaps be the catalyst needed for a turnaround. But even so, it's hard not to see this as a gamble at the moment, and I wouldn't particularly rely on it alone, considering that the EV business will arguably remain the major driving force of the company's top and bottom line as other AI initiatives are implemented over the next few years.

1. Global Structural Challenges Facing the EV Market

The first negative impact for Tesla has to do with the electric vehicle market itself.

By 2025, the world's perception of ISG, climate change, and fossil fuels have changed a lot, and with it the expectation that there would be a complete electrification of mobility, especially vehicles.

This scenario is no longer a reality or is no longer the expectation of consumers, governments, and even many automakers who, in recent months or years, have abandoned those projects that would change all their products to only electric or hybrid cars.

Some automakers have gone off the rails or projects to do away with combustion-powered products have been revived. This scenario has changed, and that idea of electric vehicle domination is behind us. That's not to say that the market is going to disappear, far from it, it will still be an important market, but not with the expectation or projection of dominance that it had just a few months ago. So this is the first impact, but not the main one.

2. Chinese Competition in the EV Landscape

The second negative impact, which is even more important than the first, has to do with competition from automakers in the electric vehicle market.

This is where Tesla is feeling it in several markets, especially in China, because there are many very competitive automakers offering much cheaper products— including some more technological ones—especially as is the case with BYD (OTCPK:BYDDY). It's worth noting that recently, the Chinese company also announced an important technological advance in battery charging, supposedly just 5 minutes, then a supercharger to charge the battery, and this competition, the technological innovation, doesn't stop.

And many of these automakers are taking important market shares. In underpenetrated markets like Latin America, the Chinese EV makers have taken over. Not yet in Europe. In the United States too, not least because of customs barriers. But this means that Tesla is suffering in several of these markets where, while it leads especially in premium cars, it doesn't have a low-cost electric car and is losing market share.

BYD has overtaken Tesla in sales, in total revenue, and should continue to gain more market share as it evolves in global markets. See the chart below illustrating Tesla's revenue growth versus BYD over the past five years. The same applies in terms of efficiency, as BYD has also managed not only to close the high operating margin gap it had versus Tesla over the past few years but to surpass them, with very solid management incorporating: (1) a vast diversification of models; (2) ever lower production costs; (3) economies of scale, with demand for EVs in China growing very strongly; and not least (4) subsidies from the Chinese government.

And Tesla even has an older product line, which is one of the criticisms of those in the EV market, that there isn't a very new vehicle to offer consumers. So yes, the competition from electric cars today is already hurting Tesla, especially from China.

3. The Politician CEO: Elon Musk's Dual Role

The third negative impact, which is, I would say, the main one, is really intriguing because it has to do with its main shareholder, its CEO, and its great leader, Elon Musk.

What was once a major asset and thus a risk for the company has now become a liability.

It's amazing how Musk's association with the right, especially since 2022, and now with the Trump administration, with supposedly the extreme right, with freedom of speech, and various other reasons, has led to Musk's image hurting Tesla.

And this is quite ironic considering that Musk has always had a leading role and carried many admirers, where he even had the idea that there was a sect of Tesla worshippers who bought vehicles for reasons such as protecting the environment, bought Tesla stock regardless of valuations, and fundamentals and revered the CEO as a revolutionary leader.

In short, it used to be a status to own a Tesla, and now it's becoming the opposite.

Musk's Anti-Woke Motivations Explained

To try to understand how this change in Elon Musk came about, I believe it all starts with Musk's own perception of the progressive 'woke' agenda.

According to Musk, 'woke' is a virus that needs to be exterminated, and he relies on a particularly traumatic experience with one of his children to have motivated him to be an avid opponent of this agenda. This is perhaps the first explanation for the change in Musk's behavior as well.

The second reason is also related to the 'woke' ideology and is freedom of expression.

Musk has been observing what is happening with social networks, with censorship, with the Big Techs, the big tech companies, especially on the big social networks, until he decided to buy Twitter, now the X platform, in 2022, as an attempt and with the aim of preserving freedom of expression. Those are his words.

And to prevent dissent from being silenced, to put an end to censorship, especially from the right, from conservatives, liberals, and libertarians, so that this platform, the internet, would be preserved as a space for debate, for the debate of ideas, which was no longer happening, in his assessment.

That was the main objective for buying Twitter, which in fact happened.

Musk and Tesla's Ties to the Republican Party

And since then, Elon Musk has become much more politically active.

This is undeniable, as he has become increasingly involved in more issues, especially in relation to freedom of expression. He has highlighted various episodes of censorship, of dissidents being persecuted, sometimes for mere opinions, persecuted by politics in various countries around the world. And as this unfolded and this reality became clearer in Musk's view, he felt that he needed to be more present in politics itself, until it came to the 2024 US presidential election.

And not only did he start supporting Trump, but he also donated hundreds of millions of dollars, and became the most important figure, President Trump's greatest influencer, to the point of taking part in the campaign, being present on stages, at rallies, giving speeches, and ended up being the great right-hand man and perhaps the main personality supporting President Trump.

And, without a doubt, his presence, and Musk's behavior, were decisive for the final result of the election.

Because of all this, Musk, of course, ended up in the middle of the road and, as continues to happen, collected many enemies.

Those who used to be on the other side of the political spectrum, more to the left, more progressive, the liberals in the U.S., began to see Musk in a different light, until Trump took office and created the famous Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE), putting Elon Musk in charge of this project, to reduce public spending, reduce waste, fraud, abuse, etc.

But it's a fact that what Musk has done, which, I believe, is excellent, is a great example for the rest of the world, of really reducing waste and cutting out what isn't needed. As he carries out all this promise with the DOGE, he is, in the meantime, antagonizing more and creating even more enmity, and not just because of the content he is doing because in the end he is cutting spending and, or he is instructing and recommending the Trump administration to cut spending, he is cutting agencies.

The DOGE has already announced that it's going to cut half the staff, and it might cut even more. So there are a lot of people who are, at the very least, disgusted or furious with what Elon Musk is doing at DOGE.

Musk’s Impact on Share Price and Market Sentiment

To better illustrate the damage to Tesla's value in 2025, the Nasdaq index fell 17% and Tesla fell 42%.

And, of course, from the all-time high that was reached right after President Trump's victory, Tesla's stock reached over $460. Since then, it has fallen by almost 50%. It's not the worst fall from an all-time high. In 2022, it fell by almost 75%, but what's striking now is the speed of that fall.

And that's why the current moment is so striking, compared to other Tesla periods, when there was also a downturn in which people sold the stock short.

Today, the short interest (around 2.9% of the float) of those who are selling the company's shares, is close to its lows. But still remain on the highest levels since early last year and way higher than back a few years ago.

There used to be a lot of disbelief about the business model and that Tesla could go bust, which almost happened in 2018. But at that time, Tesla's main customers, main investors, the base that supported the business, sales, and the company's own valuation, were supporting and embracing Elon Musk.

Now it's the other way around. Musk has managed to antagonize his main customers and investors, who are the most progressive, most to the left of the political spectrum, and not who he is allying himself with today.

And that's Musk's big dilemma because looking at the company's metrics, the valuation multiples is still quite stretched. Looking at the price over earnings of the share going forward, the projection is 90x, that's a lot.

So now there are several obstacles ahead. That's why investors see Tesla losing sales, which have already fallen by 2024. Deliveries from 2022 to 2023 rose sharply, by 1.8 million vehicles, but from 2023 to 2024 they fell slightly. The trend in 2025 is towards a further drop in market share.

Tesla's own report, its latest, which was released, also shows the Tesla market losing market share in the United States, losing it in Europe. It's still growing in China, but that's its smallest market and where it has its main competition. So it really is a delicate moment for the company. And because its valuation depends on this expectation of electric sales with a growing margin, it has almost been forced to reduce and discount the prices of its vehicles, which squeezes margins.

Before anyone argues that I'm not going to comment on robotaxis, FSD, and robotics, it's clear that Tesla's bulls expect that much, or virtually all, of Tesla's top and bottom-line growth will come from the sale of these new technologies.

However, although I won't go into these topics in depth, we can soon see that, according to some experts, Tesla is not necessarily at the same advanced level as Waymo, or other Chinese automakers. In other words, it is still debatable whether there is a clear superiority in Tesla's product.

Lastly, there is the possibility that in the future it will also dominate the robotics market with its robot, Optimus, which for the time being is not a final product, it is still being developed, but in the same way, the competition and the Chinese companies have also emerged and are trying to steal the market for this future product, which none of them really have ready to take over the world's homes.

It's important to note, however, that Tesla's valuation has almost always been based on future expectations. Some things have been delivered, others have not. Others have been postponed or continue to be postponed.

But if before there was a legion of satisfied customers, devotees, admirers, investors and willing to buy the paper, save, buy the products, and bet on the future, today this is changing. It's changing because of Musk's own attitude and behavior in recent months.

A Reverse DCF Analysis and the Margin of Safety

To better express how much would be needed for there to be some margin of safety in Tesla's investment thesis at current prices, I believe an interesting exercise would be to perform a reverse discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis to assess how realistic Tesla's share price really is.

For this analysis, I will consider a baseline for the next five years based on: (1) Tesla's D&A-to-CapEx ratio stays at 47%, and CapEx represents 12% of revenues, in line with the figures reported in the last twelve months; (2) conservatively, I will assume no changes in net working capital.

Furthermore, considering that the Wall Street consensus expects Tesla to grow revenues at a CAGR of 23% over the next five years. If I assume a discount rate of 9%, assuming a cost of equity of 8.8%, Tesla would need to report 37% in operating margins over the next five consecutive years, starting in 2025, for the company to reach an equity value of ~$823 billion—which divided between the 3.2 billion outstanding shares, would take Tesla to an implied price of ~$255 per share.

While this margin expansion is not unrealistic for some, if Tesla's Full Self-Driving (FSD) initiative could transform the company into a software-as-a-service (SaaS) business and achieve 80% margins on robotaxis—as even suggested by tech investor Cathie Wood—I think this is still speculative, and it's very unlikely that this will be the case for at least the next few years.

Therefore, I find no material evidence to suggest that there is any margin of safety in the share price of Tesla stock from this point of view.

Upside Risks to the Bearish Thesis

Elon Musk's track record as a public figure shows his resilience in the face of several periods of extreme hardship in his multiple businesses. Eventually, his involvement in politics may in fact accelerate regulatory advances around FSD, and with it, facilitate the implementation of robotaxis as quickly as possible on the streets of the U.S.—rapidly generating an expansion in the company's margins as an AI company.

If Musk's involvement in politics is indeed a move towards this goal, it could be a major home run for Tesla investors.

The Bottom Line

To what extent is Elon Musk willing to go ahead with the DOGE, making more enemies, and antagonizing his main clients and investors in order to carry out this mission to recover the U.S. economy with a zero deficit, to cut spending? How far is he willing to let Tesla bleed, which could damage not only the company, but his own personal fortune, and which could contaminate other businesses as well?

These are the dilemmas Musk faces today.

It is a great characteristic of Elon Musk's personality to get involved in controversy, to drag himself through the mud, and to sink deeper and deeper until he reaches rock bottom, in a situation that is sometimes almost irrecoverable. He's been involved in controversies throughout his public history over the last fifteen years, and at various times he's had to be helped by friends, by people he trusts, because it's a problem with his personality.

So I don't believe he won't go all the way. Even if that means Tesla losing another billion dollars in valuation. That's how he's managed to get his companies off the ground, avoid bankruptcy, and make them thrive.

Now, for those who are investing in Tesla, I would say that there is now a much bigger and more uncertain question mark than there was in the past. Although it's a point of debate for many, especially regarding Tesla's development as an AI company around FSD and robotics, I definitely don't find this not-so-new version of Elon Musk as a politician beneficial—especially associated with the Trump administration.

I also believe that, even if Tesla manages to deliver its new products such as the robotaxi in the not-so-distant future with no major delays, it will continue to struggle to win over consumers if the Tesla brand continues to be compromised by Elon Musk's image.

Therefore, as things stand, I don't believe that Tesla will be able to post substantial growth rates in revenues and margins in a short period of time (I'd say one to two years) that could justify Tesla as a Buy with an adequate margin of safety.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Bond Market Is Sending Us A New Warning
data.description:
Introduction

My regular readers will know that I follow the bond market regularly, and have written explicitly on its patterns in the past, especially as it pertains to the ongoing market narratives and the next Fed decision.

The last time I spoke about the bond market as a primary indicator for the economy, it was regarding the 10yr US Treasury Yield (US10Y), and its place in the yield curve.

The yield curve is just one of the ways that we can visualize the spreads on US Treasuries, which tells us how investors are pricing in the future. Essentially, we plot the various rates of treasuries with different maturities. Using this, we can see what investors believe rates will be at a given time in the future, signalling their beliefs in the future of the economy and markets along with it.

For reference, here is how a normal, healthy yield curve is supposed to look:

Typically, if current rates are low (which has been the norm over the last two decades), we should see a gradual increase in yields over time as investors price in more and more "time uncertainty," or the risk that something will happen that causes rates to rise in the future. The more time a treasury has left (further out on the "maturity" axis), the more chance of something adverse happening.

However, when rates are elevated from historical norms (as they are presently), the yield curve starts to get weird.

The Yield Curve

Here is our current curve, with the Federal Funds Rate shown as a highlighted band, but for reference, it is 4.25-4.50%.

The market is currently forecasting that short-term rates are higher than they will be in the medium-term, but lower than they will be in the long-term, with rates at the following points as of 4/10/25 (time of writing).

Fed Funds 4.25%
3mo 4.34%
6mo 4.17%
2yr 3.84%
5yr 4.04%
10yr 4.40%
20yr 4.90%
30yr 4.86%

One of the things that is so startling is the insidious nature of the changes in the curve. The last time I wrote directly on the yield curve was back in late December. Here is the change between then and now. Keep in mind that the Federal Funds Rate has not changed in that time.

Note that the short-mid term maturities, namely the 2yr-7yr, have moved dramatically. Even the 10yr bond is now yielding less than it was just several months ago.

Here is a chart showing the probabilities for different rates from the Fed. Notice how we're not at an 80% chance the rates are held even at the next meeting in May. It seems that even though the bond market is pricing in future cuts, and at a faster rate than before, the futures market is pricing in no rate cuts yet.

I think this is a prudent move by the Fed, for the record.

The Bear Steepener

Flashback to my last article on the 10yr yield, here's the best way I can describe a "Bear Steepener":

There are a few major takeaways to note about a bear steepener:

  • Market phenomenon where long-term yields rise faster than short-term
  • Usually indicative of high inflationary expectations
  • Caused by investors selling long-term bonds more than short-term bonds

This is due to the anticipation of much higher rates into the future. Here is the spread between the 10yr and 2yr bonds, showing the velocity of the change...

Before we look at our current chart, let's take a look at where we came from. Here is the chart I showed in that article back in late December:

Since then, it's gotten worse. Here is the updated chart to today, showing that while yields retreated some after that quick change I called out in December, they've run up again and are now far above where they were before, as seen in the changes in the yield curve and illustrated here:

The cause of a bear steepener is normally due to the Fed raising rates too slowly, and the market anticipating that their efforts won't be enough in the long run to tamp down inflation, causing the spreads to vary so widely.

However, the Fed isn't raising rates right now. It's held the same rate since December, and is on track to lower rates this year (although I think fewer than the market is predicting).

The market is predicting rate cuts at a faster and faster rate, indicating that it is pricing in an economic downturn. This tracks with the ongoing tariff chaos that has whipsawed markets in recent days, and is causing recession fears I am on the record of calling "overblown."

Despite how I feel, other players in the market are far more concrete about their recession predictions.

  • JPMorgan Model Shows Recession Fear in Markets Spiking Up to 79%
  • Goldman Sachs Sees 65% Chance of US Recession in Next Year

Add to this that now the bond market itself is pricing this in at a faster and faster rate means that we may be closer than I anticipated. I am reserving the right to change my mind, of course, but I haven't yet. For now, this panic still feels like another fleeting episode in our new volatility regime.

Expect the VIX to stay elevated, and fluctuate greatly over the next few months at least. We've already touched 52+ in the past week, a level we haven't seen since the COVID-19 crash and the GFC before that.

What Am I Doing About It?

I have been piling all of my new capital into cash positions and commodities strategies, and have even been reducing some of my equity exposure. I began this process on March 31st, after publishing this article on Trump's "Liberation Day."

To borrow from that article, since my strategy hasn't changed since I wrote that, here is how I'm positioning myself for today's markets:

While I am not in "sell everything" mode, I have become very cautious around this situation. To that end, I am not currently deploying any new capital into equities or long bonds. Instead, I am currently sitting it in cash or alternatives.

I have recently recommended a cash-like fund that I personally own, the 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (SGOV). This fund is very conservative, as it is designed to be a cash park. Cash is king right now, and the fund is currently paying me a forward yield of 4.2% to wait for the market to be done with its tantrum.

The other recommendation that I am actively buying right now is the Simplify Managed Futures ETF (CTA). This fund is effectively a manager, in this case Altis Partners sub-advises the fund, trading commodities to try to achieve total return. Through a variety of trading methods, they work to build positive track records regardless of market conditions. While they usually underperform during bull runs in stocks, these funds have historically performed best during times of uncertainty like now.

While CTA has not performed very well since I recommended it, it has still absolutely outperformed the index itself and equities in general since then. The losses seem largely to come from commodities sell-offs, as well as sell-offs in US treasuries (leading the bear steepener steepening) and Canadian treasuries. I still believe that the fund will outperform the market over the medium-term, and believe it to be the best investment in our current market environment until all the tariff business is settled.

Here is CTA against the market YTD:

Conclusion

Ultimately, we are seeing a worsening of expectations from the bond market, leading to a new warning for investors in the form of a deeper "bear steepener," a technical pattern we can illustrate with the dramatic widening of the 10yr and 2yr US Treasuries. The velocity of this change is worsening, and indicating that investors expect a recession in the US at a much higher probability than they did when I discussed the last time we saw this warning, back in December.

Now, investors are faced with a tough choice on how to proceed in markets that are as volatile as 2020 or 2008, and the bond market seems to be pricing in deep cuts to the central rate coming soon. While I don't share the bond market's direct views, believing that the Fed will act more prudently than expected, and is willing to tolerate more market pain than the market itself believes, I am not buying equities.

Instead, I am currently pushing cash into commodities strategies via Simplify's CTA and cash, waiting for the chaos to subside and the economic picture for the US to become more clear. Patience is prudent currently, as even the labor market and inflation data indicate that the Fed has no reason to cut rates.

Thanks for reading.
